utterances.transcript: My name is Adi and I am one of the co founders of AgentMail.
It is the inbox API to give to your OpenAI,
to your Hermes,
to your Claude Code,
to your Codex, to any single AI agent you build. Now, I put my landing page up there because
at first glance, it seems like this new idea, and we seem like a new company.
Email inboxes for AI agents. But I hate to break it to you. I'm kind of an unk in this space.
I've been thinking about this since November
And
being very, very honest with you, we didn't have much traction until
earlier this year when, uh, lobster themed harness sort of took over the world out of nowhere.
And
amid all the sign ups, amid everyone saying, okay, it's very obvious to me why an agent needs its own email inbox,
I kept getting one criticism
that just kept getting on my nerves,
and I am going to show you that tweet right now.
It's,
I came across agent mail and they require a human to first create an account using their Google,
using their email address to give my agent its own email address. It's the biggest epic fail I've ever seen in my life.
And it's kind of funny, we spent all this time thinking about an agent actually using the product,
we never thought about how they would sign up. So,
what I want you guys to do, I've spent the last week or last few weeks
making sure you can sign up for Agent Mail with a single prompt. So if you have your Claude code, if you have your codex on your phone right now, try it with me. Let's do a live demo.
Amazing.
There we go.
Let's go. We're live.
So type with me,
get yourself
an
agent inbox
by visiting
agent.email.
So now, I use codex because
Claude's super restrictive. He doesn't like what I do on it sometimes.
But
all you have to do is type this prompt in and your agent can
visit
this website,
read a skill.md file,
and sign up. So you'll see it here,
it is going to sign up. I don't even see a browser.
I'm just waiting on Codex to kind of do its thing.
And it says
it's a very minimal page.
All it's gonna do is tell an agent to read a skill.md file and follow that accordingly.
So it's gonna do that.
I'm gonna wait on it.
Someone play the Jeopardy music while my codex is running.
Cool.
Awesome.
So now you can see
that this
actually went ahead and provisioned an inbox for me to go
right away. So you might ask me like, hey, Adi,
how do you prevent spam and how do you make sure
that
every single agent can't just go haywire with their inbox.
And this is what we call a compromise.
So what we do is we'll provision the resources
right away, we'll make sure your agent gets an inbox, but we'll put a very very strict allow list on it that says,
hey,
these are you can only send and receive to a certain address
and you can't send more than 10 emails per day to spare my inbox and your guys' inbox from everything these agents are gonna do.
So
the talk of this is email as an identity layer. And I think when we think about agent mail,
I
think about email being the existing primitive that's really defined the Internet as it is. So think about your inbox.
Every single action,
reaction,
transaction,
it all lands in your inbox, whether it's your tax records, whether it's your invoices,
whether it's a receipt or a claim. So what we want to do is instead of building a brand new standard,
we'll let the good folks at WorkOS do that, We wanna give better access to the existing primitives of today. So if I tell my agent, go sign up for Substack
using your
agent mail address
and spin up a browser,
it can go ahead and do this completely autonomously
without needing a human in the loop. And I think there's so many different use cases
where

an agent is going to need its own email inbox just as an audit trail, just to make it as contextually aware as possible, and just allow it to access the same interface as we live on. Whether it's pairing something with an agent card to capture receipts,
transactions,
whether it is
emailing you and being cc'd in any thread, becoming your personal assistant,
helping you coordinate, helping you schedule,
you name it. So, thank you so much guys for the time. If you have any questions, I'll be sticking around and really appreciate you. Thank you so much.

sentences.text: My name is Adi and I am one of the co founders of AgentMail.
It is the inbox API to give to your OpenAI, to your Hermes, to your Claude Code, to your Codex, to any single AI agent you build.
Now, I put my landing page up there because at first glance, it seems like this new idea, and we seem like a new company.
Email inboxes for AI agents.
But I hate to break it to you.
I'm kind of an unk in this space.
I've been thinking about this since November 2022.
And being very, very honest with you, we didn't have much traction until earlier this year when, uh, lobster themed harness sort of took over the world out of nowhere.

And amid all the sign ups, amid everyone saying, okay, it's very obvious to me why an agent needs its own email inbox, I kept getting one criticism that just kept getting on my nerves, and I am going to show you that tweet right now.
It's, I came across agent mail and they require a human to first create an account using their Google, using their email address to give my agent its own email address.
It's the biggest epic fail I've ever seen in my life.
And it's kind of funny, we spent all this time thinking about an agent actually using the product, we never thought about how they would sign up.
So, what I want you guys to do, I've spent the last week or last few weeks making sure you can sign up for Agent Mail with a single prompt.
So if you have your Claude code, if you have your codex on your phone right now, try it with me.
Let's do a live demo.
Amazing.
There we go.
Let's go.
We're live.
So type with me, get yourself an agent inbox by visiting agent.email.
So now, I use codex because Claude's super restrictive.
He doesn't like what I do on it sometimes.
But all you have to do is type this prompt in and your agent can visit this website, read a skill.md file, and sign up.
So you'll see it here, it is going to sign up.
I don't even see a browser.
I'm just waiting on Codex to kind of do its thing.
And it says it's a very minimal page.
All it's gonna do is tell an agent to read a skill.md file and follow that accordingly.
So it's gonna do that.
I'm gonna wait on it.
Someone play the Jeopardy music while my codex is running.
Cool.
Awesome.
So now you can see that this actually went ahead and provisioned an inbox for me to go right away.
So you might ask me like, hey, Adi, how do you prevent spam and how do you make sure that every single agent can't just go haywire with their inbox.
And this is what we call a compromise.

So what we do is we'll provision the resources right away, we'll make sure your agent gets an inbox, but we'll put a very very strict allow list on it that says, hey, these are you can only send and receive to a certain address and you can't send more than 10 emails per day to spare my inbox and your guys' inbox from everything these agents are gonna do.
So the talk of this is email as an identity layer.
And I think when we think about agent mail, I think about email being the existing primitive that's really defined the Internet as it is.
So think about your inbox.
Every single action, reaction, transaction, it all lands in your inbox, whether it's your tax records, whether it's your invoices, whether it's a receipt or a claim.
So what we want to do is instead of building a brand new standard, we'll let the good folks at WorkOS do that, We wanna give better access to the existing primitives of today.
So if I tell my agent, go sign up for Substack using your agent mail address and spin up a browser, it can go ahead and do this completely autonomously without needing a human in the loop.

And I think there's so many different use cases where an agent is going to need its own email inbox just as an audit trail, just to make it as contextually aware as possible, and just allow it to access the same interface as we live on.

Whether it's pairing something with an agent card to capture receipts, transactions, whether it is emailing you and being cc'd in any thread, becoming your personal assistant, helping you coordinate, helping you schedule, you name it.
So, thank you so much guys for the time.
If you have any questions, I'll be sticking around and really appreciate you.
Thank you so much.

alternatives.transcript: My name is Adi and I am one of the co founders of AgentMail. It is the inbox API to give to your OpenClaw, to your Hermes, to your ClawdCode, to your Codex, to any single AI agent you build. Now, I put my landing page up there because at first glance, it seems like this new idea, and we seem like a new company. Email inboxes for AI agents. But I hate to break it to you. I'm kind of an unk in this space. I've been thinking about this since November 2024. And being very, very honest with you, we didn't have much traction until earlier this year when, uh, lobster themed harness sort of took over the world out of nowhere. And amid all the sign ups, amid everyone saying, okay, it's very obvious to me why an agent needs its own email inbox, I kept getting one criticism that just kept getting on my nerves, and I am going to show you that tweet right now. It's, I came across agent mail and they require a human to first create an account using their Google, using their email address to give my agent its own email address. It's the biggest epic fail I've ever seen in my life. And it's kind of funny, we spent all this time thinking about an agent actually using the product, we never thought about how they would sign up. So, what I want you guys to do, I've spent the last week or last few weeks making sure you can sign up for Azure Mail with a single prompt. So if you have your Claude code, if you have your codex on your phone right now, try it with me. Let's do a live demo. Amazing. There we go. Let's go. We're live. So type with me, get yourself an agent inbox by visiting agent dot email. So now, I use codex because Claude's super restrictive. He doesn't like what I do on it sometimes. But all you have to do is type this prompt in and your agent can visit this website, read a skill dot m d file, and sign up. So you'll see it here, it is going to sign up. I don't even see a browser. I'm just waiting on Codex to kind of do its thing. And it says it's a very minimal page. All it's gonna do is tell an agent to read a skill dot md file and follow that accordingly. So it's gonna do that. I'm gonna wait on it. Someone play the Jeopardy music while my codex is running. Cool. Awesome. So now you can see that this actually went ahead and provisioned an inbox for me to go right away. So you might ask me like, hey, Adi, how do you prevent spam and how do you make sure that every single agent can't just go haywire with their inbox. And this is what we call a compromise. So what we do is we'll provision the resources right away, we'll make sure your agent gets an inbox, but we'll put a very very strict allow list on it that says, hey, these are you can only send and receive to a certain address and you can't send more than 10 emails per day to spare my inbox and your guys' inbox from everything these agents are gonna do. So the talk of this is email as an identity layer. And I think when we think about agent mail, I think about email being the existing primitive that's really defined the Internet as it is. So think about your inbox. Every single action, reaction, transaction, it all lands in your inbox, whether it's your tax records, whether it's your invoices, whether it's a receipt or a claim. So what we want to do is instead of building a brand new standard, we'll let the good folks at WorkOS do that, We wanna give better access to the existing primitives of today. So if I tell my agent, go sign up for Substack using your agent mail address and spin up a browser, it can go ahead and do this completely autonomously without needing a human in the loop. And I think there's so many different use cases where an agent is going to need its own email inbox just as an audit trail, just to make it as contextually aware as possible, and just allow it to access the same interface as we live on. Whether it's pairing something with an agent card to capture receipts, transactions, whether it is emailing you and being cc'd in any thread, becoming your personal assistant, helping you coordinate, helping you schedule, you name it. So, thank you so much guys for the time. If you have any questions, I'll be sticking around and really appreciate you. Thank you so much.

paragraphs.transcript:
My name is Adi and I am one of the co founders of AgentMail. It is the inbox API to give to your OpenClaw, to your Hermes, to your ClawdCode, to your Codex, to any single AI agent you build. Now, I put my landing page up there because at first glance, it seems like this new idea, and we seem like a new company. Email inboxes for AI agents. But I hate to break it to you.

I'm kind of an unk in this space. I've been thinking about this since November 2024. And being very, very honest with you, we didn't have much traction until earlier this year when, uh, lobster themed harness sort of took over the world out of nowhere. And amid all the sign ups, amid everyone saying, okay, it's very obvious to me why an agent needs its own email inbox, I kept getting one criticism that just kept getting on my nerves, and I am going to show you that tweet right now. It's, I came across agent mail and they require a human to first create an account using their Google, using their email address to give my agent its own email address.

It's the biggest epic fail I've ever seen in my life. And it's kind of funny, we spent all this time thinking about an agent actually using the product, we never thought about how they would sign up. So, what I want you guys to do, I've spent the last week or last few weeks making sure you can sign up for Azure Mail with a single prompt. So if you have your Claude code, if you have your codex on your phone right now, try it with me. Let's do a live demo.

Amazing. There we go. Let's go. We're live. So type with me, get yourself an agent inbox by visiting agent dot email.

So now, I use codex because Claude's super restrictive. He doesn't like what I do on it sometimes. But all you have to do is type this prompt in and your agent can visit this website, read a skill dot m d file, and sign up. So you'll see it here, it is going to sign up. I don't even see a browser.

I'm just waiting on Codex to kind of do its thing. And it says it's a very minimal page. All it's gonna do is tell an agent to read a skill dot md file and follow that accordingly. So it's gonna do that. I'm gonna wait on it.

Someone play the Jeopardy music while my codex is running. Cool. Awesome. So now you can see that this actually went ahead and provisioned an inbox for me to go right away. So you might ask me like, hey, Adi, how do you prevent spam and how do you make sure that every single agent can't just go haywire with their inbox.

And this is what we call a compromise. So what we do is we'll provision the resources right away, we'll make sure your agent gets an inbox, but we'll put a very very strict allow list on it that says, hey, these are you can only send and receive to a certain address and you can't send more than 10 emails per day to spare my inbox and your guys' inbox from everything these agents are gonna do. So the talk of this is email as an identity layer. And I think when we think about agent mail, I think about email being the existing primitive that's really defined the Internet as it is. So think about your inbox.

Every single action, reaction, transaction, it all lands in your inbox, whether it's your tax records, whether it's your invoices, whether it's a receipt or a claim. So what we want to do is instead of building a brand new standard, we'll let the good folks at WorkOS do that, We wanna give better access to the existing primitives of today. So if I tell my agent, go sign up for Substack using your agent mail address and spin up a browser, it can go ahead and do this completely autonomously without needing a human in the loop. And I think there's so many different use cases where an agent is going to need its own email inbox just as an audit trail, just to make it as contextually aware as possible, and just allow it to access the same interface as we live on. Whether it's pairing something with an agent card to capture receipts, transactions, whether it is emailing you and being cc'd in any thread, becoming your personal assistant, helping you coordinate, helping you schedule, you name it.

So, thank you so much guys for the time. If you have any questions, I'll be sticking around and really appreciate you. Thank you so much.

sentences.text: My name is Adi and I am one of the co founders of AgentMail.
It is the inbox API to give to your OpenClaw, to your Hermes, to your ClawdCode, to your Codex, to any single AI agent you build.
Now, I put my landing page up there because at first glance, it seems like this new idea, and we seem like a new company.
Email inboxes for AI agents.
But I hate to break it to you.
I'm kind of an unk in this space.
I've been thinking about this since November 2024.
And being very, very honest with you, we didn't have much traction until earlier this year when, uh, lobster themed harness sort of took over the world out of nowhere.

And amid all the sign ups, amid everyone saying, okay, it's very obvious to me why an agent needs its own email inbox, I kept getting one criticism that just kept getting on my nerves, and I am going to show you that tweet right now.
It's, I came across agent mail and they require a human to first create an account using their Google, using their email address to give my agent its own email address.
It's the biggest epic fail I've ever seen in my life.
And it's kind of funny, we spent all this time thinking about an agent actually using the product, we never thought about how they would sign up.
So, what I want you guys to do, I've spent the last week or last few weeks making sure you can sign up for Azure Mail with a single prompt.
So if you have your Claude code, if you have your codex on your phone right now, try it with me.
Let's do a live demo.
Amazing.
There we go.
Let's go.
We're live.
So type with me, get yourself an agent inbox by visiting agent dot email.
So now, I use codex because Claude's super restrictive.
He doesn't like what I do on it sometimes.
But all you have to do is type this prompt in and your agent can visit this website, read a skill dot m d file, and sign up.
So you'll see it here, it is going to sign up.
I don't even see a browser.
I'm just waiting on Codex to kind of do its thing.
And it says it's a very minimal page.
All it's gonna do is tell an agent to read a skill dot md file and follow that accordingly.
So it's gonna do that.
I'm gonna wait on it.
Someone play the Jeopardy music while my codex is running.
Cool.
Awesome.
So now you can see that this actually went ahead and provisioned an inbox for me to go right away.
So you might ask me like, hey, Adi, how do you prevent spam and how do you make sure that every single agent can't just go haywire with their inbox.
And this is what we call a compromise.

So what we do is we'll provision the resources right away, we'll make sure your agent gets an inbox, but we'll put a very very strict allow list on it that says, hey, these are you can only send and receive to a certain address and you can't send more than 10 emails per day to spare my inbox and your guys' inbox from everything these agents are gonna do.
So the talk of this is email as an identity layer.
And I think when we think about agent mail, I think about email being the existing primitive that's really defined the Internet as it is.
So think about your inbox.
Every single action, reaction, transaction, it all lands in your inbox, whether it's your tax records, whether it's your invoices, whether it's a receipt or a claim.
So what we want to do is instead of building a brand new standard, we'll let the good folks at WorkOS do that, We wanna give better access to the existing primitives of today.
So if I tell my agent, go sign up for Substack using your agent mail address and spin up a browser, it can go ahead and do this completely autonomously without needing a human in the loop.

And I think there's so many different use cases where an agent is going to need its own email inbox just as an audit trail, just to make it as contextually aware as possible, and just allow it to access the same interface as we live on.

Whether it's pairing something with an agent card to capture receipts, transactions, whether it is emailing you and being cc'd in any thread, becoming your personal assistant, helping you coordinate, helping you schedule, you name it.
So, thank you so much guys for the time.
If you have any questions, I'll be sticking around and really appreciate you.
Thank you so much.
